text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic D-III lymph node dissection, preserving the left colic artery and sigmoid artery during the procedure for rectal cancer treatment.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic exploration, dissociating the retroperitoneum from the right side of the mesocolon. The Toltz gap is entered along the right side of the inferior mesenteric artery, followed by the dissection of apical lymph node number 253.

The surgeon dissociates and exposes the inferior mesenteric artery and vein, as well as the left colic artery. Apical lymph node number 253 is dissected. The branches of the inferior mesenteric vein are ligated and incised to protect the left colic artery.

The surgeon ligates and incises the inferior mesenteric vein, followed by dissociation and exposure of the first sigmoid artery. The inferior mesenteric artery is ligated and incised below the left colic artery. Gauze is placed to protect the left ureter and gonadal vessels during the procedure. The surgeon separates sigmoid adhesions and dissociates the sigmoid mesocolon.

The surgeon dissociates the retro-rectal space, followed by the right and left pararectal spaces. The right intestinal wall is exposed, along with the anterior rectal wall and left intestinal wall, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the rectal cancer procedure.

The surgeon incises the rectum at the pre-resect position and the sigmoid mesocolon. The specimen is extracted from the incision of the protective ileostomy. An enteric and intestinal anastomosis is performed, followed by suturing to strengthen the anastomosis.

The surgeon places a drainage tube and creates a stoma at the incision site. The specimen from the total mesorectal excision is displayed, with visible characteristics indicating pathologic staging of PT-III-N-I-M-Zero and one apical lymph node metastasis.
